ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bread food product class; no specific cheese bread loaf subclass exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and rolls as a category. No specific cheese bread loaf code exists; this is the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cheese bread loaf; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is one slice of cheese bread loaf weighing approximately 40 grams, providing around 100 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Cheese Bread Loaf — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cheese Bread Loaf (cr_id F05020017), confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white bread and enriched bread analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese bread loaf in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is estimated from white bread analogues (GI range 70–78) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). Cheese enrichment and moderate fibre (3.1g/100g) may marginally lower GI but the refined wheat base dominates. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (75 × 32.4g carbs per 100g × 40g serving) / 100 / 100 = 9.7g. Input record GI confidence was 0.441 (low); this estimate is retained as analogous_estimate with appropriate documentation.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content at 524 mg per 100g, with cheese and added salt as primary contributors
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit cheese bread loaf to one slice (40g, ~210mg sodium) per serving and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods such as processed meats, butter, or salty spreads to keep total meal sodium within the recommended 600mg per meal threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 75) and high carbohydrate density (32.4g/100g) from refined wheat flour base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict cheese bread loaf to one thin slice (30–40g) per occasion, pair it with a protein source such as eggs or legumes to blunt postprandial glucose response, and prefer wholemeal or high-fibre bread alternatives with a GI below 55 for routine consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat of approximately 4.8g per 100g from cheese and butter, combined with high sodium (524mg/100g) and a small trans fat contribution (0.1g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, cheese bread loaf should be consumed occasionally and in small portions (one slice, 40g), as the saturated fat and sodium content can contribute to LDL elevation and blood pressure burden; choose reduced-fat cheese variants and pair with unsaturated fat sources such as avocado rather than butter.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat (~4.8g/100g) and trans fat (0.1g/100g) from cheese and margarine, combined with a pro-inflammatory refined wheat base
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit cheese bread loaf due to its pro-inflammatory saturated and trans fat content; if consumed, choose versions made with olive oil instead of margarine or butter, and prefer sourdough or wholegrain bases to reduce glycaemic load and systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese bread loaf is a soft, enriched white bread product in which cheese is incorporated directly into the dough before baking, producing a moist, tender crumb with a mild savoury flavour and a lightly golden crust. The bread is typically made from refined wheat flour, water, yeast, salt, sugar, butter or margarine, and a blend of shredded or processed cheese such as cheddar or Edam. The cheese melts into the dough during baking, contributing to the characteristic soft texture, slight richness, and elevated protein and fat content compared to plain white bread. Per 100g, cheese bread loaf provides 249 kcal, 10.3g protein, 8.65g fat (of which approximately 4.8g is saturated), 32.4g carbohydrates, 3.1g dietary fibre, 3.8g sugar, 524mg sodium, 127mg calcium, and 0.46mg iron. The high sodium content (524mg/100g) reflects the combined contribution of cheese and added salt in the dough formulation, making this a high-sodium bread product. The glycemic index is estimated at 75 (high), driven by the refined wheat flour base, and the glycemic load per 40g slice is approximately 9.7g. Clinically, cheese bread loaf is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension due to its sodium load, unsuitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its high GI and carbohydrate density, and of moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and sodium. It is commonly consumed at breakfast or as a snack in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, often eaten plain, toasted, or with spreads. The calcium content (127mg/100g) from the cheese component provides a meaningful contribution to daily calcium intake. This food is classified as a processed grain product and contains gluten and dairy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a slice of cheese bread?
Is cheese bread high in sodium?
What is the GI of cheese bread?
Is cheese bread loaf suitable for hypertension?
How much protein is in cheese bread?
What allergens are in cheese bread loaf?
Can I eat cheese bread if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020017) with a confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is estimated from analogous white bread data in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021, which is an approved GI source; confidence for GI is medium due to the analogous estimate basis. Overall record confidence is rated high based on the strength of the primary nutrient source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for cheese bread loaf is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F05020017. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.1g/100g based on the health_suitability input note referencing margarine as a possible ingredient; this is an estimate and should be verified against the specific product formulation. 3. potassium_mg is set to 110mg/100g as an estimate based on USDA SR Legacy data for enriched white bread with cheese; this value is not directly reported in the HPB source and should be treated as approximate. 4. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item. 5. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate from white bread data (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement for cheese bread loaf was found in the approved GI source. Input record GI confidence was 0.441. 6. saturated_fat_g of 4.8g/100g is estimated from the health_suitability input note; the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 7. The dietary_tag 'high_fibre' in the input cultural section is borderline — 3.1g/100g meets the Singapore HPB threshold for 'source of fibre' but not 'high fibre' (which requires ≥6g/100g); this tag has been noted but not propagated to dietary_flags. 8. This food is not a Singaporean-origin dish requiring mandatory multilingual naming; it is classified as Western/International, so local_names fields are null and G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied by English alone.
